metadata.note: Ours: in-process certified prover (browser-identical), median of 5 release runs; `ours_proof_bytes` is the size of OUR certified artifact — the SR proof for the pigeonhole class, the compact GF/ℤ-ring linear certificate for the algebraic families — measured by streaming it through a byte counter in memory (never written to disk, capped so a pathological proof can't exhaust RAM). External solvers: subprocess via `timeout` on the byte-identical DIMACS, each emitting a clausal proof to disk whose size is `proof_bytes` (solve+certify). Z3: in-process oracle (no proof). CryptoMiniSat runs in its default DRAT-emitting config; with its GF(2) Gaussian enabled it would decide parity fast but could not emit a standard clausal proof. 20=UNSAT, 124=timeout. No extrapolation; SaDiCaL given 45s to run to completion. NOTE: sub-millisecond times are dominated by external process startup, so the headline claims rest on the cases where competitors take seconds or hit the wall (where startup is negligible).

families.name: Pigeonhole PHP(n)
families.mechanism: certified SR symmetry-breaking proof (Heule–Kiesl–Biere class), proof size n(n-1)/2
families.separation: Z3 and Kissat (resolution) hit the 2^Ω(n) wall (Haken 1985) and cannot finish; SaDiCaL completes but our certified SR proof is several× faster with a kilobyte-scale certificate vs its megabytes
families.note: Our general prover, handed the same CNF with no hint, decides PHP by matching in microseconds; and SDCL auto-discovers the certified proof with zero hints: PHP(5)=40 clauses in 23ms; PHP(6)=60 clauses in 91ms; PHP(7)=84 clauses in 290ms;

rows.ours_detail: certified SR proof: 120 steps (= n(n-1)/2), machine-checked
certified SR proof: 231 steps (= n(n-1)/2), machine-checked
certified SR proof: 378 steps (= n(n-1)/2), machine-checked
certified SR proof: 561 steps (= n(n-1)/2), machine-checked
certified SR proof: 780 steps (= n(n-1)/2), machine-checked

families.name: Pigeonhole variants (functional / onto / weak)
families.mechanism: one generic prover, three pigeonhole variants — each decided by matching/symmetry
families.separation: above the resolution wall (holes ≥ 16), so Z3, Kissat, CaDiCaL and CryptoMiniSat all time out; SaDiCaL's symmetry machinery completes but the generic prover refutes each variant in milliseconds — the same matching/symmetry insight, three different encodings
families.note: Functional PHP forbids a pigeon in two holes; onto PHP demands every hole filled (a bijection); weak PHP overloads the holes with surplus pigeons. Different encodings, one contradiction — the pigeonhole principle — recognized structurally with no problem-specific hint, at sizes where every resolution engine hits the 2^Ω(n) wall.

rows.ours_detail: counting certificate: 18 pigeons > 17 holes (O(1)) — functional PHP(18): + “each pigeon in ≤1 hole”
counting certificate: 20 pigeons > 19 holes (O(1)) — onto PHP(20): bijection — every hole filled
counting certificate: 24 pigeons > 18 holes (O(1)) — weak PHP(24→18): 24 pigeons into 18 holes

families.name: Mutilated chessboard (side × side)
families.mechanism: generic prover → maximum-matching Hall witness (same CNF as the others)
families.separation: exponential for resolution (Alekhnovich 2004): by 18×18 Z3, Kissat AND SaDiCaL all hit the wall — ours decides by the colour-count Hall argument in microseconds
families.note: Remove two opposite same-colour corners: a colour-count Hall witness, found in microseconds on sparse, irregular adjacency — not just complete-bipartite pigeonhole.

rows.ours_detail: generic prover → Hall witness over 176 dominoes (matching)
generic prover → Hall witness over 360 dominoes (matching)
generic prover → Hall witness over 608 dominoes (matching)

families.name: Clique-colouring K_n (n−1 colours)
families.mechanism: generic prover → matching/counting: k-colouring K_n is pigeonhole (vertices vs colours)
families.separation: χ(K_n) = n, so n−1 colours cannot properly colour K_n — a PHP-class contradiction resolution refutes only exponentially (Z3/Kissat wall), decided here by the O(1) counting bound through the colouring encoding
families.note: Adjacent vertices of the complete graph must differ in colour, so each colour is an at-most-one clique over all n vertices — exactly pigeonhole with n items and k = n−1 slots. The matching detector recognizes it with no problem-specific hint.

rows.ours_detail: K_16 needs 16 colours, 15 given → counting certificate (16 > 15), 240 vars
K_24 needs 24 colours, 23 given → counting certificate (24 > 23), 552 vars
K_30 needs 30 colours, 29 given → counting certificate (30 > 29), 870 vars

families.name: Tseitin parity (3-regular expander)
families.mechanism: Gaussian elimination over GF(2) — certified 0=1 refutation
families.separation: the second mechanism: Gaussian over GF(2) is flat in microseconds while Z3 walls and Kissat/SaDiCaL scale into seconds with megabyte proofs
families.note: Parity on an expander graph is hard by graph-expansion, not covering symmetry (Ben-Sasson–Wigderson). It is P via linear algebra — a different algebra, same philosophy.

rows.ours_detail: GF(2) Gaussian: 70-equation 0=1 refutation, certified
GF(2) Gaussian: 90-equation 0=1 refutation, certified
GF(2) Gaussian: 110-equation 0=1 refutation, certified

families.name: Tseitin on a bounded-treewidth grid (width 12)
families.mechanism: Gaussian elimination over GF(2) — certified 0=1 refutation
families.separation: a grid has treewidth 12 (bounded), so a polynomial-size resolution proof EXISTS — yet Kissat, CaDiCaL and CryptoMiniSat all time out on the parity, unable to find it without Gaussian reasoning; GF(2) decides it in milliseconds
families.note: The strongest indictment of resolution search: unlike the expander (where the hardness is fundamental), here a short proof is KNOWN to exist and the field still cannot find it. Bounded treewidth does not save a solver that cannot do the algebra.

rows.ours_detail: GF(2) Gaussian on a 12×80 grid (treewidth 12): 960-equation 0=1 refutation, certified — a polynomial proof exists yet the field walls
GF(2) Gaussian on a 12×120 grid (treewidth 12): 1440-equation 0=1 refutation, certified — a polynomial proof exists yet the field walls
GF(2) Gaussian on a 12×160 grid (treewidth 12): 1920-equation 0=1 refutation, certified — a polynomial proof exists yet the field walls

families.name: Coupled exactly-one + parity
families.mechanism: fused parity + cardinality — GF(2) Gaussian × exactly-one bound, decided as one
families.separation: a MIXED obstruction no single theory can cut: pure GF(2) parity is blind (the parity chain is satisfiable) and pure matching is blind (exactly-one is satisfiable) — only reasoning about both at once refutes it
families.note: Exactly-one of the selectors forces an odd count; the parity closure forces even — a contradiction visible only when the two theories are fused. A scalable mixed family (2n variables) where a single-theory solver must fall back to search.

rows.ours_detail: fused parity+cardinality: exactly-one (odd) vs GF(2) parity (even), 40 vars — certified refutation
fused parity+cardinality: exactly-one (odd) vs GF(2) parity (even), 80 vars — certified refutation
fused parity+cardinality: exactly-one (odd) vs GF(2) parity (even), 120 vars — certified refutation

families.name: Mod-3 Tseitin — GF(3) expander
families.mechanism: Gaussian elimination over GF(p) — the parity cut carried to every prime
families.separation: resolution-hard (Z3 & Kissat blow up) and invisible to a GF(2) parity engine — only GF(3) decides it; the one-hot CNF widens with p, so the field walls at smaller n
families.note: Mod-3 counting on a 3-regular expander: total charge 2 ≢ 0 (mod 3) but ≡ 0 (mod 2), so a GF(2) parity engine returns SAT — blind. GF(3) Gaussian refutes it in microseconds with a checkable certificate; SaDiCaL's PR machinery is blind to it too.

rows.ours_detail: GF(3) Gaussian: 30-equation linear-dependency refutation (0 ≡ r ≢ 0), certified
GF(3) Gaussian: 40-equation linear-dependency refutation (0 ≡ r ≢ 0), certified
GF(3) Gaussian: 50-equation linear-dependency refutation (0 ≡ r ≢ 0), certified

families.name: Mod-5 Tseitin — GF(5) expander
families.mechanism: Gaussian elimination over GF(p) — the parity cut carried to every prime
families.separation: resolution-hard (Z3 & Kissat blow up) and invisible to a GF(2) parity engine — only GF(5) decides it; the one-hot CNF widens with p, so the field walls at smaller n
families.note: Mod-5 counting on a 3-regular expander: total charge 2 ≢ 0 (mod 5) but ≡ 0 (mod 2), so a GF(2) parity engine returns SAT — blind. GF(5) Gaussian refutes it in microseconds with a checkable certificate; SaDiCaL's PR machinery is blind to it too.

rows.ours_detail: GF(5) Gaussian: 20-equation linear-dependency refutation (0 ≡ r ≢ 0), certified
GF(5) Gaussian: 30-equation linear-dependency refutation (0 ≡ r ≢ 0), certified
GF(5) Gaussian: 40-equation linear-dependency refutation (0 ≡ r ≢ 0), certified

families.name: Mod-7 Tseitin — GF(7) expander
families.mechanism: Gaussian elimination over GF(p) — the parity cut carried to every prime
families.separation: resolution-hard (Z3 & Kissat blow up) and invisible to a GF(2) parity engine — only GF(7) decides it; the one-hot CNF widens with p, so the field walls at smaller n
families.note: Mod-7 counting on a 3-regular expander: total charge 2 ≢ 0 (mod 7) but ≡ 0 (mod 2), so a GF(2) parity engine returns SAT — blind. GF(7) Gaussian refutes it in microseconds with a checkable certificate; SaDiCaL's PR machinery is blind to it too.

rows.ours_detail: GF(7) Gaussian: 20-equation linear-dependency refutation (0 ≡ r ≢ 0), certified
GF(7) Gaussian: 30-equation linear-dependency refutation (0 ≡ r ≢ 0), certified
GF(7) Gaussian: 40-equation linear-dependency refutation (0 ≡ r ≢ 0), certified

families.name: Mod-6 Tseitin — ℤ/6 ring (CRT)
families.mechanism: Gaussian elimination over ℤ/m via CRT — the parity cut carried to a composite modulus
families.separation: resolution-hard (one-hot over 6 values per edge) and invisible to a GF(2) parity engine; the ℤ/6 ring engine decides it through its GF(3) factor by CRT — microseconds, with a checkable certificate
families.note: Mod-6 counting on a 3-regular expander: total charge 2 ≢ 0 (mod 6) but ≡ 0 (mod 2). By CRT ℤ/6 ≅ GF(2) × GF(3); GF(2) is blind (2 ≡ 0) yet GF(3) refutes it (2 ≢ 0), so the composite ring decides what a pure parity engine cannot.

rows.ours_detail: ℤ/6 Gaussian (CRT via GF(3)): 12-equation linear-dependency refutation, certified
ℤ/6 Gaussian (CRT via GF(3)): 18-equation linear-dependency refutation, certified
ℤ/6 Gaussian (CRT via GF(3)): 24-equation linear-dependency refutation, certified

families.name: Modular counting — Count₃(n)
families.mechanism: GF(3) modular counting — an exact 3-partition of an n-set, decided by the mod-3 cut
families.separation: an exact 3-partition exists iff 3 | n, so Count₃(n) is UNSAT exactly when 3 ∤ n — a mod-3 counting fact resolution cannot make at low width, decided directly over GF(3)
families.note: The cleanest statement of the modular-counting principle. The mod-p Tseitin families scale the same GF(p) rung to a resolution wall; here it is the legible combinatorial statement, small by the C(n,q) encoding.

rows.ours_detail: Count_3(4): 4 items cannot split into blocks of 3 (3∤4) — mod-3 counting cut, 4 vars
Count_3(7): 7 items cannot split into blocks of 3 (3∤7) — mod-3 counting cut, 35 vars
Count_3(8): 8 items cannot split into blocks of 3 (3∤8) — mod-3 counting cut, 56 vars

families.name: Linear ordering principle GT(n)
families.mechanism: ordering specialist — a strict total order with no maximum, refuted from the structure
families.separation: GT(n) asserts a strict total order in which every element has a greater one — impossible, since a finite total order has a maximum. Our specialist certifies it in polynomial time; the general cascade and resolution solvers search super-polynomially
families.note: A canonical resolution-stress family with no matching or parity to exploit — it pins whether the engine can recognize the ordering structure itself. Ours does, deciding GT(n) directly where search walls.

rows.ours_detail: ordering specialist: a strict total order on 20 elements must have a maximum, contradicting 'no maximum' — refuted from the structure, 400 vars
ordering specialist: a strict total order on 30 elements must have a maximum, contradicting 'no maximum' — refuted from the structure, 900 vars
ordering specialist: a strict total order on 40 elements must have a maximum, contradicting 'no maximum' — refuted from the structure, 1600 vars

families.name: Random 3-SAT (control)
families.separation: no structural advantage: ours tracks Kissat (the tuned CDCL baseline) within a few ms; SaDiCaL, built for structure, fares poorly on random SAT — we claim no win here
families.note: The honesty control: no symmetry, no cardinality, no parity to exploit; small times here are confounded by external process startup.

rows.ours_detail: plain CDCL: sat — general engine, not our forte
plain CDCL: unsat — general engine, not our forte
